# Attribution Patterns — Detailed Research Notes

Attribution manipulation corrupts the record of who caused a conversion. It is different from fake demand: the customer may be real, but credit is stolen or misassigned.

## Common attribution patterns

### Click flooding

A source generates many weak attribution signals so that later organic or unrelated installs are incorrectly credited to it.

### Install hijacking

A fraudulent signal appears at the moment an install occurs, allowing the wrong party to capture credit.

### Cookie stuffing

A tracking identifier is placed without genuine referral influence so a later purchase is credited to the wrong affiliate.

### Coupon last-click capture

A coupon or deal property captures last-click attribution late in the buying journey, even when it did not create the original demand.

## What researchers should ask

- Did the credited source actually influence the user?
- Is the time between click and install plausible?
- Do click volume and post-install behaviour match?
- Are conversions unusually clustered around last-click intercepts?
- Do internal analytics disagree with partner reporting?

## Ethical risk

Attribution manipulation diverts money from honest partners and corrupts future budget allocation.
